Extract: - Labeled fields: identify `Label: value` patterns, emit (label, value) pairs - Section boundaries: detect Epic section headers (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, A/P, Signature) - Freetext runs: narrative text between labels/sections - Preserve source position information so findings can map back to the original text for rendering - Define data structures for parsed output: a sequence of typed spans (LabeledField, SectionHeader, Narrative, etc.) each carrying their text content and source offset - Plain text extraction path: strip all HTML, preserve line breaks and section structure - `apcd/src/apctp_parse.rs` — unit tests: feed synthetic fixture HTML through parser, assert correct extraction of labeled fields, section boundaries, and narrative spans ## Verification - Unit tests pass via `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` - Feed the synthetic fixture through the parser. Print structured output showing detected labels, sections, and narrative spans with positions. All labeled PHI fields (Patient, DOB, MRN, Attending, Facility, etc.) correctly identified as LabeledField spans. # jjezs_pace ₢A9AAE ## Character The intellectual core. Data acquisition, algorithm implementation, and clinical content gating. Requires attention to the anchored/matched vocabulary distinction and case normalization. ## Deliverables **Dictionaries:** - `dictionaries/surnames.txt` — top 1,000 US surnames from Census 2010 data (public domain) - `dictionaries/firstnames.txt` — top 1,000 US first names from SSA baby names (public domain) - `dictionaries/cities.txt` — US state capitals + top 100 cities by population (public domain) - `dictionaries/medical_whitelist.txt` — ~500 curated medical terms: common medications, lab tests, diagnoses, anatomy terms, medical abbreviations - `dictionaries/english_whitelist.txt` — top 5,000 common English words **`apcd/src/apcrd_dictionaries.rs`:** - Load dictionary files at startup - Build `aho-corasick` automaton from lowercase blacklist entries (surnames + firstnames + cities) - Build whitelist hash sets from lowercase medical + English terms - Case-insensitive lookups: input tokens lowercased before matching, original case preserved for display - Expose lookup API: is_blacklisted, is_whitelisted, classify_word **`apcd/src/apcrm_match.rs`:** - Tier 1 regex patterns: SSN, phone, email, dates (multiple formats), street addresses, zip codes, MRN/account/encounter following labels. Known ambiguities accepted (zip matching 5-digit numbers, date matching version numbers) — conservative bias, clinician reviews. - Tier 2 label-anchored extraction: per-word anchoring — each word following a known Epic label is individually flagged RED. These are **anchored** items, independent of any dictionary. Anchored classification is final. - Tier 3 dictionary scan: aho-corasick pass with case-insensitive matching, whitelist filter, collision → YELLOW. These are **matched** items. **`apcd/src/apcre_engine.rs`:** - Orchestrate: take parsed spans from `apcrp`, run all three tiers, merge by highest severity (anchored RED > matched YELLOW > PASS) - Output: classified token stream — each token carries its text, classification (RED/YELLOW/PASS), mechanism (anchored/matched/regex), PHI type label (NAME, DATE, MRN, PHONE, etc.), and source position - DOB→age transform: when a date is labeled DOB, compute age from current date. Ages >89 emit `Age: 90+` per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)). - Clinical content heuristic: scan for 2+ known clinical labels (Patient:, DOB:, MRN:, etc.) to gate processing. Non-clinical content returns a diagnostic (content length, type, first ~100 chars) for debugging. Clipboard cleared after successful consumption. **Unit tests:** - `apctd_dictionaries.rs` — dictionary loading, case-insensitive lookup, blacklist/whitelist/collision classification - `apctm_match.rs` — regex patterns against known examples (SSN, phone, dates, addresses), label-anchored per-word extraction, anchored vs matched precedence - `apcte_engine.rs` — full pipeline with synthetic fixture: all PHI caught, all medical terms pass, DOB→age with >89 handling, clinical heuristic acceptance/rejection ## Verification - All unit tests pass via `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` - Feed synthetic fixture through full pipeline (parse → detect → classify). Print findings list showing every detected PHI item with tier (anchored/matched/regex), severity, category label, and matched text. All names, dates, identifiers, addresses, phones in the fixture caught. Medical terms, medications, lab values, vitals pass clean. # jjezs_pace ₢A9AAF ## Character UX implementation. HTML/CSS/JS frontend work in the Tauri webview, wired to Rust backend results via Tauri IPC. ## Deliverables - `apcd/ui/index.html` updated: three states: 1. **Initial state**: "Ann's PHI Clipbuddy" title + "Copy a patient note from Epic, then switch to this window" instruction 2. **Non-clinical clipboard**: diagnostic line (content length, type, first ~100 chars), returns to instruction state 3. **Triage view**: two-pane layout — document preview (top/left) and findings panel (bottom/right) - `apcd/ui/style.css` updated: RED (`background: #ff000030; border: 2px solid red`), YELLOW (`background: #ffff0030; border: 2px solid orange`), GREY (`color: #999`) inline highlight styles - `apcd/ui/app.js` updated: receive classified token stream from Rust backend via Tauri `invoke`, render document preview with inline highlights, populate findings panel - Tauri IPC commands: `consume_clipboard` (triggered on window focus), returns classified token stream as JSON or non-clinical diagnostic - Findings panel with **two sections**: - **Questionable Findings** (upper, YELLOW): dictionary matches and ambiguous detections. Default ELIDE, easily toggled to PASS. - **Definite PHI** (lower, RED): label-anchored and regex-matched identifiers. Default ELIDE, toggleable to PASS but separated to create deliberate friction. - Each entry shows: matched text, PHI category label, [ELIDE ▼] / [PASS ▼] toggle - Toggle changes update the document preview in real time - Clipboard change detection on refocus: unchanged clipboard preserves triage state ## Verification Load fixture → focus app → see the synthetic Epic note rendered with names in red boxes, questionable words in yellow boxes, medical terms and clinical content in grey. YELLOW findings in upper panel section, RED in lower. Toggle a yellow finding to PASS — preview updates. Toggle a red finding to PASS — requires scrolling to lower section. Refocus without clipboard change — triage preserved. Visual confirmation against the synthetic fixture that all PHI is highlighted and all clinical content passes clean. # jjezs_pace ₢A9AAG ## Character Pipeline completion. Wire the "Copy Anonymized" button to produce clean text from the triage state. ## Deliverables - `apcre_engine.rs` updated: generate anonymized plain text from classified token stream respecting current ELIDE/PASS state - Category-specific placeholders: `[NAME]`, `[DATE]`, `[MRN]`, `[PHONE]`, `[ADDRESS]`, `[EMAIL]`, `[SSN]`, `[FACILITY]`, `[PROVIDER]`, `[ACCOUNT]`, `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` - DOB→age transform: dates labeled as DOB emit `Age: NN` instead of `[DATE]`. Ages >89 emit `Age: 90+` per HIPAA Safe Harbor. - PASS items (including RED items toggled to PASS by clinician): emit original text unchanged - ELIDE items: emit placeholder for their PHI category - Preserve line breaks and section structure from parsed output — plain text should be readable, not a wall - Tauri IPC command: `copy_anonymized` — takes current toggle state from frontend, generates clean text, writes to clipboard via `arboard::set_text()` - Frontend: "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard" button at bottom of window, calls `copy_anonymized` ## Verification Load fixture → triage view appears → toggle a few yellow items to PASS → toggle one red item to PASS → click "Copy Anonymized" → paste into a text editor. Verify: all ELIDE items replaced with category placeholders, DOB replaced with computed age (90+ for >89), toggled PASS items preserved as original text, section structure readable, no PHI leaks. Text should be suitable for pasting into Open Evidence. # jjezs_pace ₢A9AAH ## Character Infrastructure and platform integration. Two independent pieces: self-update mechanism in the app, and the deploy tabtarget that ships to Ann's machine. ## Deliverables **Self-update (`apcd/src/apcru_update.rs`):** - Watch `/Users/Shared/apcua/` using `notify` crate (FSEvents on macOS) - On new `.app` bundle detected: copy over current app bundle, spawn `open -n` on new path, exit current process - No confirmation dialog — fully automatic - Graceful handling: if watch directory doesn't exist, create it silently and keep watching - Run watcher on a background thread, independent of main Tauri event loop **Deploy tabtarget (`tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh`):** - `cargo tauri build` (release) - `scp` the `.app` bundle to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/` - Report success with bundle size and path **Integration:** - Wire `apcru_update` into `apcap_main.rs` — start watcher on app launch - Update `apck-claude-context.md` with deploy workflow documentation ## Verification 1. Build and run app locally. Confirm watcher starts (log message). 2. Copy a dummy `.app` to `/Users/Shared/apcua/` — app detects it (may not self-replace locally since paths differ, but detection fires). 3. `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` completes: bundle appears on Ann's machine at `/Users/Shared/apcua/`. 4. End-to-end: Ann launches app, we deploy an update, app auto-restarts with new version.

toolUseResult.stdout: # jjezs_paddock ₣A9 ## Character Product design into prototype implementation. Starts with synthesis (specs capturing a rich design conversation), transitions to exploratory engineering (Tauri + Rust + cross-platform clipboard), then steady implementation of a three-tier PHI detection engine. Requires careful attention to minting discipline — this kit lives alongside RBK/JJK/BUK and must respect the prefix universe. ## Problem Clinicians using Epic EHR need to consult Open Evidence (an AI diagnostic tool) but must not paste Protected Health Information (PHI) into it. HIPAA Safe Harbor defines 18 PHI categories that must be removed. Today, clinicians either skip the consultation or manually scrub text — both are unacceptable. No clipboard-level tool exists to automate the triage. ## Solution Ann's PHI Clipbuddy (`apcap`) — a Tauri desktop app (macOS, later Windows) that: 1. Validates clipboard content as clinical via heuristic, clears system clipboard after consumption 2. Parses Epic "Copy All" HTML for structural context (labels, sections) 3. Runs a three-tier PHI detection engine (regex → label-anchored → dictionary) 4. Displays a triage view: RED (definite PHI), YELLOW (questionable), GREY (pass) 5. Lets clinician toggle findings — YELLOW easily, RED with deliberate friction (separate panel section) 6. Writes anonymized plain text to clipboard with category-specific placeholders (DOB→age, >89→"90+") 7. Copy a dummy `.app` to `/Users/Shared/apcua/` — app detects it (may not self-replace locally since paths differ, but detection fires). 3. `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` completes: bundle appears on Ann's machine at `/Users/Shared/apcua/`. 4. End-to-end: Ann launches app, we deploy an update, app auto-restarts with new version.

Here's the dependency picture:

```
AAB scaffold ──→ AAC fixture+clipboard ──→ AAD parsing ──→ AAE detection ──→ AAF frontend ──→ AAG anonymize
                                                                                              AAH self-update (needs AAB only)
```

Most paces chain through the Rust infrastructure that AAB creates. But two deliverables are **pure data** with zero code dependency:

1. **Synthetic Epic fixture** (`test_fixtures/epic_progress_note.html`) — listed under ₢A9AAC. This is a hand-crafted HTML file representing Epic "Copy All" output with realistic PHI across all 18 HIPAA categories. No Rust, no Cargo, no Tauri needed.

2. **Dictionary files** (5 files in `dictionaries/`) — listed under ₢A9AAE. Curating surname/firstname/city blacklists and medical/English whitelists from public domain sources. Pure text files, no code dependency.

Both are on the critical path (the fixture gates all integration testing, the dictionaries gate Tier 3 detection), so doing them now shortens the overall timeline.

The fixture is probably the higher-value target — it requires clinical domain knowledge to make realistic, and every subsequent pace uses it for verification. The dictionaries are more mechanical (download Census/SSA data, curate medical terms).

Want me to start on either of those? The fixture would need to be slated as its own pace or split from ₢A9AAC, since that pace also includes Rust clipboard code that depends on AAB.

content.content: 34 35 Epic's "Copy All" places HTML on the clipboard with structural formatting: 36 37 - `Label: value` patterns for demographics and metadata 38 - Section headers for clinical categories (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, Assessment/Plan) 39 - Narrative freetext in the History of Present Illness and other sections 40 - Signature blocks with provider names and timestamps 41 42 The HTML structure is semi-structured — not arbitrary prose and not fully machine-readable. file.content:
Output: classified token stream → frontend
```

### Tier 1 — Regex Patterns (→ RED)

Structural patterns with low false positive rate. No database dependency.

**Known ambiguities:** The zip code regex (`\d{5}`) can match 5-digit lab values, identifiers, or dosage amounts. The date regex can match version numbers or other formatted numerics. The street address regex can match non-address text with number-word-suffix patterns. These are accepted risks for the prototype — the conservative bias (false positive > false negative) means these appear as RED items the clinician can review. Context-aware refinement is deferred.

| Pattern | Category | Regex (representative) | Notes |
|---------|----------|----------------------|-------|
| SSN | `[SSN]` | `\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}` | |
| Phone | `[PHONE]` | `\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{4}`, `\(\d{3}\)\s?\d{3}-\d{4}` | Multiple formats |
| Email | `[EMAIL]` | `[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+` | |
| Dates | `[DATE]` | `\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{2,4}`, `\d{1,2}-\d{1,2}-\d{2,4}`, month name patterns | Multiple formats |
| Street address | `[ADDRESS]` | `\d+\s+[A-Z]\w+\s+(St\|Dr\|Ave\|Blvd\|Rd\|Ln\|Way\|Ct\|Pl\|Cir\|Drive\|Street\|Avenue\|Boulevard\|Road\|Lane\|Court\|Place\|Circle)` | Followed by optional city/state/zip |
| Zip code | `[ADDRESS]` | `\b\d{5}(-\d{4})?\b` | Within address context |
| MRN (labeled) | `[MRN]` | Value following `MRN:` label | |
| Account (labeled) | `[ACCOUNT]` | Value following `Account` or `Acct` label | |
| Encounter ID (labeled) | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | Value following `Encounter ID:` label | |

content.content:
105	
106	Dates receive special handling: when a date follows a `DOB:` label, the engine computes age from the current date and emits `Age: NN` instead of `[DATE]` in the anonymized output. Per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)), ages over 89 are aggregated: computed age >89 emits `Age: 90+`.
107	
108	### Tier 2 — Label-Anchored Extraction (→ RED)
109	
110	Words immediately following known Epic labels are PHI regardless of dictionary membership. The label is authoritative — if Epic says `Patient: Xylophone McZephyr`, that's a name even if no database contains it.
111	
112	| Label Pattern | Category | Extraction Rule |
113	|--------------|----------|-----------------|
114	| `Patient:` | `[NAME]` | All text until next label or line break |
115	| `Attending:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break |
116	| `Provider:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break |
117	| `Referring:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break |
118	| `Facility:` | `[FACILITY]` | All text until next label or line break |
119	| `Electronically signed by:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break |
120	| `Emergency contact` | `[NAME]` | Name portion, phone extracted by Tier 1 |
121	| `primary care physician` | `[PROVIDER]` | Following name (e.g., "Dr. Susan Chen") |
122	
123	Label matching is case-insensitive on the label text. Value extraction extends to the next structural boundary (label, line break, or HTML element boundary).
124	
125	**Per-word anchoring:** Each word in the extracted value is individually flagged as RED. "Margaret J. Thornton" after `Patient:` produces three separate **anchored** items, each classified `[NAME]`. In anonymized output: `[NAME] [NAME] [NAME]`. No multi-word merge is attempted.
126	
127	Items caught by Tier 2 are **anchored** — their RED classification is final. They are not subsequently dictionary-checked. If a word is both anchored (Tier 2) and matched (Tier 3), the anchored classification takes precedence.
128	
129	### Vocabulary: Anchored vs. Matched

file.content:
Dates receive special handling: when a date follows a `DOB:` label, the engine computes age from the current date and emits `Age: NN` instead of `[DATE]` in the anonymized output. Per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)), ages over 89 are aggregated: computed age >89 emits `Age: 90+`.

### Tier 2 — Label-Anchored Extraction (→ RED)

Words immediately following known Epic labels are PHI regardless of dictionary membership. The label is authoritative — if Epic says `Patient: Xylophone McZephyr`, that's a name even if no database contains it.

| Label Pattern | Category | Extraction Rule |
|--------------|----------|-----------------|
| `Patient:` | `[NAME]` | All text until next label or line break |
| `Attending:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break |
| `Provider:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break |
| `Referring:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break |
| `Facility:` | `[FACILITY]` | All text until next label or line break |
| `Electronically signed by:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break |
| `Emergency contact` | `[NAME]` | Name portion, phone extracted by Tier 1 |
| `primary care physician` | `[PROVIDER]` | Following name (e.g., "Dr. Susan Chen") |

Label matching is case-insensitive on the label text. Value extraction extends to the next structural boundary (label, line break, or HTML element boundary).

**Per-word anchoring:** Each word in the extracted value is individually flagged as RED. "Margaret J. Thornton" after `Patient:` produces three separate **anchored** items, each classified `[NAME]`. In anonymized output: `[NAME] [NAME] [NAME]`. No multi-word merge is attempted.

Items caught by Tier 2 are **anchored** — their RED classification is final. They are not subsequently dictionary-checked. If a word is both anchored (Tier 2) and matched (Tier 3), the anchored classification takes precedence.

### Vocabulary: Anchored vs. Matched

content.text: Now I have a thorough understanding of what the fixture needs to exercise. Let me check the HIPAA 18 categories from the product spec to make sure I cover all of them.

content.content:
29	## HIPAA Safe Harbor — The 18 Categories
30	
31	The HIPAA Privacy Rule provides two de-identification methods. This tool implements the **Safe Harbor method** (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)), which requires removal of 18 specified identifier categories. Reference: [HHS De-Identification Guidance](https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/special-topics/de-identification/index.html).
32	
33	The following identifiers must be removed for Safe Harbor de-identification:
34	
35	1. Names
36	2. Geographic data smaller than state (addresses, zip codes with <20k population)
37	3. Dates more specific than year (birth, admission, discharge, death); ages over 89 aggregated to single category (90+)
38	4. Phone numbers
39	5. Fax numbers
40	6. Email addresses
41	7. Social Security numbers
42	8. Medical record numbers
43	9. Health plan beneficiary numbers
44	10. Account numbers
45	11. Certificate/license numbers
46	12. Vehicle identifiers
47	13. Device identifiers/serial numbers
48	14. Web URLs
49	15. IP addresses
50	16. Biometric identifiers
51	17. Full-face photographs (not applicable for text)

file.content:
## HIPAA Safe Harbor — The 18 Categories

The HIPAA Privacy Rule provides two de-identification methods. This tool implements the **Safe Harbor method** (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)), which requires removal of 18 specified identifier categories. Reference: [HHS De-Identification Guidance](https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/special-topics/de-identification/index.html).

The following identifiers must be removed for Safe Harbor de-identification:

1. Names
2. Geographic data smaller than state (addresses, zip codes with <20k population)
3. Dates more specific than year (birth, admission, discharge, death); ages over 89 aggregated to single category (90+)
4. Phone numbers
5. Fax numbers
6. Email addresses
7. Social Security numbers
8. Medical record numbers
9. Health plan beneficiary numbers
10. Account numbers
11. Certificate/license numbers
12. Vehicle identifiers
13. Device identifiers/serial numbers
14. Web URLs
15. IP addresses
16. Biometric identifiers
17. Full-face photographs (not applicable for text)

content.text: Good. Now let me create the fixture. I need to cover: - All exercisable HIPAA categories (1-15, 18; skip 16-17 which are biometric/photo) - All Tier 2 label patterns (Patient, Attending, Provider, Referring, Facility, Electronically signed by, Emergency contact, primary care physician) - Narrative text with embedded names/addresses for Tier 3 testing - Rich clinical content that must survive (labs, vitals, meds, diagnoses, procedures, abbreviations) - `Label: value` HTML structure - A DOB that tests both normal age and the >89 edge case - 2+ clinical labels for heuristic gating

Referring: Susan Chen, MD
Visit Date: 04/09/2026
Reason for Consult:
Geriatric medicine consultation requested for cognitive assessment and fall risk evaluation in the setting of acute hospitalization for NSTEMI.
History of Present Illness:
Mr. Eriksen is a 93-year-old male admitted 04/07/2026 with NSTEMI, now post-catheterization with single-vessel PCI to LAD. Consultation requested by Dr. Whitfield for pre-discharge cognitive and functional assessment. The patient's daughter, Karen Eriksen-Moody, reports progressive memory difficulties over the past 18 months — missed appointments, repeated questions, and difficulty managing medications. She states her brother, Thomas Eriksen of 15 Ocean View Road, Cape Elizabeth, ME 04107, has also noticed the changes during weekly phone calls. The patient lives alone at 42 Pinehurst Drive since the death of his wife, Miriam Eriksen, in 2024. A home health aide, Patricia Nowak, visits three times weekly. Prior cognitive screening by Dr. Susan Chen on 01/15/2026 noted MoCA 22/30 with deficits in delayed recall and visuospatial domains.
Past Medical History:
1. Coronary artery disease — NSTEMI 04/2026, s/p PCI to LAD
2. Hypertension
3. Atrial fibrillation — rate controlled, on apixaban
4. Mild cognitive impairment — MoCA 22/30 (01/2026)
5. Benign prostatic hyperplasia
6. Osteoporosis — T-score -2.8 lumbar spine
7. Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss — uses hearing aids
8. S/P appendectomy 1961
Medications (Home):
1. Apixaban (Eliquis) 5mg PO BID
2. Metoprolol succinate (Toprol-XL) 50mg PO daily
3. Amlodipine 5mg PO daily
4. Atorvastatin 40mg PO QHS
5. Tamsulosin (Flomax) 0.4mg PO QHS
6. Alendronate (Fosamax) 70mg PO weekly
7. Vitamin D3 2000 IU PO daily
8. Aspirin 81mg PO daily — added post-PCI
9. Clopidogrel 75mg PO daily — added post-PCI
Vitals (04/09/2026 08:15):
BP: 132/78 mmHg | HR: 72 bpm (irregular) | RR: 16 /min | Temp: 97.8 F | SpO2: 95% RA
Weight: 68.5 kg | Height: 175 cm | BMI: 22.4
Cognitive Assessment:
MoCA: 20/30 (decline from 22/30 on 01/15/2026)
- Visuospatial/Executive: 3/5
- Naming: 3/3
- Attention: 5/6
- Language: 2/3
- Abstraction: 1/2
- Delayed Recall: 2/5 (registered 5/5)
- Orientation: 4/6 (missed day of week, date)

GDS-15: 4/15 (mild depressive symptoms)
Timed Up and Go: 18.2 seconds (elevated fall risk, cutoff >12s)
Grip Strength: 22 kg right, 20 kg left (below normative for age/sex)
ADLs: Independent in bathing, dressing, toileting. Requires cueing for medication management.
IADLs: Requires assistance with finances, transportation, meal preparation. Independent in telephone use.
Assessment/Plan:
93-year-old male with mild cognitive impairment progressing toward early dementia, now post-NSTEMI with increased fall risk and polypharmacy concerns.

1. Cognitive Decline — MoCA 20/30, down from 22 three months ago. Pattern suggests amnestic MCI progressing. Recommend outpatient neuropsych testing with Dr. Rebecca Langford at Maine Neuropsychology Associates, 75 John Roberts Road, South Portland, ME 04106, phone 207-555-0671. Consider cholinesterase inhibitor (donepezil 5mg QHS) after cardiology clears — discuss with Dr. Whitfield re: bradycardia risk given recent ACS.

2. Fall Risk — TUG 18.2s, grip strength below normative. Recommend PT for balance and strengthening program. Home safety evaluation by OT. Remove loose rugs, install grab bars in bathroom at 42 Pinehurst Drive. Night lights in hallway and bathroom.

3. Polypharmacy — Currently on 9 medications. Reviewed with clinical pharmacist Angela Kim, PharmD. No immediate deprescribing targets given acute ACS requiring DAPT. Reassess tamsulosin and alendronate at 3-month follow-up — fall risk vs benefit.

4. Caregiver Support — Daughter Karen Eriksen-Moody is primary caregiver at distance. Brother Thomas Eriksen is local but limited involvement. Current home health aide 3x/week may be insufficient. SW referral for Area Agency on Aging services. Discussed with Karen at 207-555-0488 — she will explore increased aide hours.

5. Advance Care Planning — Patient has DPOA (Karen Eriksen-Moody) but no living will on file. Recommend goals of care conversation prior to discharge given cognitive trajectory and cardiac disease burden. Palliative care consult if patient/family amenable.

6. Follow-up — Geriatric medicine clinic in 4-6 weeks. Repeat MoCA at that visit. PCP follow-up with Dr. Chen within 2 weeks of discharge.
Electronically signed by: Naomi Okwu, MD — 04/09/2026 14:33

content.text:
Notched `a5a04122`. The parallel chat is clearly mid-scaffold — all the BUK dispatch and tabtarget files are untracked.

**Remaining in ₢A9AAC:**
- `apcal_main.rs` — fixture loader binary (arboard clipboard write)
- `apcap_main.rs` update — clipboard read on focus, raw display
- Clipboard change detection (byte-compare on refocus)
- Wiring `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh` to run `apcal`

All of that is Rust + bash, blocked by the scaffold pace.

**On tabtarget naming:** I'd push back slightly on encoding numbers in the colophon. The BUK pattern for this is **imprint** — same colophon, different target baked into the filename:

```
tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.progress.sh
tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.geriatric.sh
```

Same pattern as `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh` / `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.srjcl.sh`. The colophon `apcw-fl` stays terminal, the imprint names the fixture. Tab-completing `tt/apcw-fl<TAB>` shows both options.

Alternatively, **param1 channel** — single tabtarget, fixture name as argument:
```
tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh progress
tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh geriatric
```

Imprint feels right here since there are only two fixtures and they're stable targets. Param1 would be better if fixtures were frequently added. Preference?

message.content: Ah imprint is fine.  Are you inclined to reslate given our progress?

content.text: Yes. The docket as-written doesn't mention the geriatric fixture at all, and the fixture loader tabtarget needs to become imprint-channeled for two targets instead of one. Let me reslate.

input.content:
# jjezs_reslate A9AAC

## Character

Integration work — two independent pieces (fixture loader + clipboard consumption) that meet in the middle.

## Deliverables

**Fixtures (DONE):**
- `test_fixtures/epic_progress_note.html` — synthetic Epic "Copy All" HTML with full PHI coverage across 15 of 18 HIPAA categories. Demographics header, narrative HPI with embedded names/addresses, vitals, labs, medications, assessment/plan, signature block.
- `test_fixtures/epic_geriatric_consult.html` — geriatric consult exercising the age >89 rule (DOB 11/02/1932, age 93 must emit "Age: 90+"). Lighter PHI footprint, cognitive assessment scores that must survive, brand/generic medication pairs.

**Fixture loader (`apcd/src/apcal_main.rs`):**
- Uses `arboard` to write HTML file contents to clipboard as HTML format
- Takes fixture file path as argument

**Tabtargets (imprint channel — one per fixture):**
- `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.progress.sh` — loads `epic_progress_note.html` onto clipboard
- `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.geriatric.sh` — loads `epic_geriatric_consult.html` onto clipboard
- Colophon `apcw-fl`, channel `imprint`

**Clipboard consumption (`apcap_main.rs` update):**

content.text:
The other independent work is **dictionary curation** from ₢A9AAE — five text files in `dictionaries/`, all pure data, no Rust dependency.

Here's a prompt to paste into a fresh chat:

---

Mount A9AAE. We are working on the dictionary files ONLY — the Rust code in this pace is blocked by earlier paces. Focus exclusively on creating these five files under `Tools/apck/apcd/dictionaries/`:

1. `surnames.txt` — top 1,000 US surnames from Census 2010 data (public domain)
2. `firstnames.txt` — top 1,000 US first names from SSA baby names (public domain)
3. `cities.txt` — US state capitals + top 100 cities by population (public domain)
4. `medical_whitelist.txt` — ~500 curated medical terms: common medications, lab tests, diagnoses, anatomy terms, medical abbreviations
5. `english_whitelist.txt` — top 5,000 common English words

All entries one-per-line, lowercase, no duplicates, sorted alphabetically. The blacklists (surnames, firstnames, cities) will feed an aho-corasick automaton. The whitelists (medical, english) will be hash sets. Case normalization happens at lookup time in Rust — store everything lowercase in the files.

Check the two test fixtures in `Tools/apck/test_fixtures/` to ensure the fictional PHI names (Thornton, Eriksen, Whitfield, Anand, Vasquez, etc.) appear in the dictionaries where appropriate — surnames like Thornton and Washington should be in the surname list since they're common Census names. Fictional names that wouldn't be in the top 1,000 (Eriksen, Okwu, Kowalski) correctly won't match — that's fine, those are caught by Tier 2 label anchoring.

Also ensure the medical whitelist covers terms appearing in the fixtures: medication names (metformin, lisinopril, atorvastatin, apixaban, clopidogrel, etc.), lab tests (troponin, BNP, HbA1c, etc.), diagnoses (hypertension, NSTEMI, diabetes, etc.), and abbreviations (BID, QHS, PRN, etc.).

Notch against A9AAE when the files are created. Do NOT touch any Rust code or bash scripts.

---
